About: It is about a board game.
AssociatedWith: It is associated with the mancala family of board games.
It is associated with Ashanti origin.
It is associated with the Caribbean.
It is associated with teaching arithmetic to African children.
BelongsTo: It belongs to the mancala family of board games.
ConsistsOf: It consists of an oware board.
It consists of two straight rows of six pits called houses.
It consists of optionally one large score house at each end.
HasA: It has two straight rows of six pits.
It has a score house at each end.
It has four seeds in each of the twelve smaller houses at the start.
It has a hinged cover like a diptych.
It has scoring houses carved into the two halves of the cover.
HasFeatureOf: It is characterized by slight variations as to the layout of the game, number of players and strategy of play.
It is characterized by being played worldwide.
It is characterized by being an abstract strategy game.
It is characterized by having many names.
It is characterized by rules for the abapa variation.
It is characterized by a board with two straight rows of six pits called houses.
It is characterized by optionally having one large score house at each end.
It is characterized by starting with four seeds in each of the twelve smaller houses.
It is characterized by boards that may be elaborately carved or simple and functional.
It is characterized by boards that may include a pedestal or be hinged for portability.
HeldIn: It was held in the Bono Region of Ghana.
It was held in the Bono East Region of Ghana.
It was held in the Ahafo Region of Ghana.
It was held in the Central Region of Ghana.
It was held in the Western Region of Ghana.
It was held in the Eastern Region of Ghana.
It was held in the Ashanti Region of Ghana.
It was held in the Caribbean.
It was held in South India.
IsA: It is an abstract strategy game.
It is a pit and pebble game.
It is a member of the mancala family of board games.
It is considered the national game of Bono State.
It is considered the national game of Ashanti City-State.
It is considered the national game of Antigua & Barbuda.
It is a social two-player abstract.
KnownAs: It is known as ayò.
It is known as ayoayo.
It is known as awalé.
It is known as wari.
It is known as ouri.
It is known as ouril.
It is known as uril.
It is known as warri.
It is known as Pallanguzhi.
It is known as wali.
KnownFor: It is known for its uncertain origin.
It is known for being widely believed to be of Ashanti origin.
It is known for having many names and variations.
It is known for the abapa variation.
It is known for its objective to capture more seeds than one's opponent.
It is known for its sowing process.
It is known for its capturing rules.
It is known for its "Grand slam" variations.
It is known for reflecting traditional African values.
It is known for its role in teaching arithmetic to African children.
MemberOf: It is a member of the mancala family of board games.
OriginatedIn: It originated in the Ashanti region.
PartOf: It is part of the mancala family of board games.
PublishedAs: It was published as the board game Oh-Wah-Ree in 1964 by 3M.
UsedAs: It is used as a focus for entertainment.
It is used as a means for meeting others.
UsedFor: It is used for teaching arithmetic to African children.
It is used for serious, adult play.
It is used for recreational play.
It is used for meeting others.
It is used for teaching arithmetic.
UsedIn: It is used in the Caribbean.
It is used in South India.
UsedTo: It is used to capture more seeds than one's opponent.
It is used to win the game by capturing 25 seeds.
It is used to end the game in a draw with 24 seeds each.
It is used to distribute seeds in a process called sowing.
It is used to capture seeds.
It is used to allow the opponent to continue playing.
It is used to force a draw with best play.
